explanation: The sentence states that the user can disable or enable the leak compensation feature at any time. Translating this into LTL, we identify that enabling or disabling is a user action (enableLeakCompensation). The leak compensation being active is represented by leakCompensationActive. 'At any time' suggests the user can always (globally) perform this action and see the effect. Therefore, globally, it should always be possible (i.e., eventually) to enable or disable leak compensation; that is, from any state, it is always possible to reach a state where the user has enabled or disabled the feature. Assuming two atomic propositions for the actions—enableLeakCompensation (user enables) and !enableLeakCompensation (user disables)—and that leakCompensationActive reflects the system state, a general LTL for such a requirement is 'GF enableLeakCompensation & GF !enableLeakCompensation'. This specifies that, infinitely often, it is possible to enable, and infinitely often, it is possible to disable, the feature. This captures the ability to switch states at any time.

explanation: The sentence means that, at any time, the user should be able to either enable or disable the leak compensation feature. This can be mapped in LTL as: at all times, it is always possible to enable or disable leak compensation. In LTL, being able to perform an action at any time corresponds to the temporal property that it is always possible (i.e., 'globally, eventually') for the user to enable or disable the feature. That is, at every point in time, eventually the property can be made true. Since the propositions 'enableLeakCompensation' and disabling can be mapped respectively, and the action of disabling can be interpreted as 'not enableLeakCompensation' (if there's not a specific atomic proposition for disable), we take the LTL formula to say: 'globally, it is always possible to eventually enableLeakCompensation' AND 'globally, it is always possible to eventually disable leak compensation'. If there are no explicit enable/disable atomic propositions and only a proposition indicating whether leak compensation is active (leakCompensationActive), then 'enable' means making it active, 'disable' means making it inactive. So 'the user can enable at any time' means: globally, eventually leakCompensationActive can be achieved (GF leakCompensationActive), and 'the user can disable at any time' means: globally, eventually not leakCompensationActive can be achieved (GF !leakCompensationActive). Both should hold, so the final formula is (GF leakCompensationActive) & (GF !leakCompensationActive).

This states that at any point, in the future, it is always possible to enable or disable leak compensation. explanation: The natural language sentence states: 'The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time.' We first identify what actions or atomic propositions are involved in enabling/disabling the leak compensation feature. Among the provided atomic propositions, 'enableLeakCompensation' indicates that the user enables the leak compensation, and its negation (¬enableLeakCompensation) would correspond to the user disabling it. The phrase 'at any time' implies that it is always possible for the user to perform these actions; that is, at every point in time, it is possible for the user to cause either enabling or disabling the feature in the future. In LTL, this is usually modeled as: it is always the case (G) that eventually (F) the user can enable OR disable the feature. This can be written as G(F(enableLeakCompensation) | F(!enableLeakCompensation)), but to directly express the ability to toggle the feature at any time, the stronger requirement is that at each point in time, both enabling and disabling are possible in the future: G(F(enableLeakCompensation) & F(!enableLeakCompensation)). However, depending on the context, sometimes it's sufficient to state that both are always eventually possible: GF(enableLeakCompensation) & GF(!enableLeakCompensation), which means 'enable' and 'disable' both occur infinitely often. But to model the 'at any time' user ability more faithfully, G(F(enableLeakCompensation)) & G(F(!enableLeakCompensation)) is best, i.e., from any moment, it's always possible to eventually enable or disable. Final LTL: G(F(enableLeakCompensation)) & G(F(!enableLeakCompensation))

For LTL, to capture 'can be enabled/disabled at any time', we want to express that it is always possible (using 'G' and 'F') to eventually (F) set leak compensation active (enable) or inactive (disable). For atomic propositions, we have 'enableLeakCompensation' to indicate the user enables it, and 'leakCompensationActive' to indicate the feature is active. Since disable is the negation of 'leakCompensationActive' (assuming only two states), the user must be able to either enable or disable at any time. Thus: Globally (at all times), it must be possible (through some next steps) for the user to enable or disable leak compensation: G(F enableLeakCompensation | F !leakCompensationActive). Also, to closely match the intent, it's common to use GF to express 'infinitely often' (that at any time you can eventually enable and disable leak compensation), so we use GF enableLeakCompensation and GF !leakCompensationActive to express that enabling and disabling is always available. This implies that, at every point in time, it is always possible for the user to next enable or disable leak compensation. Since the atomic propositions provided include only 'enableLeakCompensation', but do not have a distinct atomic for 'disable', we assume toggling can be expressed as enabling or not enabling (i.e., the ability to enable or not enable whenever desired). Therefore, for all times, it is possible in the next instant to enable leak compensation. Symmetrically, if there were an atomic for disabling, we would also assert that at all times, in the next instant, it is possible to disable. Since 'leakCompensationActive' signals activity, we interpret user control as the ability to force 'enableLeakCompensation' at any next instant. The LTL formula expressing this is G(F(enableLeakCompensation)), meaning: at all times, it will always be possible (eventually, and therefore possibly at the very next time or further in the future) to enable leak compensation. If both enable and disable atomic propositions were available, we would write G(F(enableLeakCompensation)) & G(F(disableLeakCompensation)), but as only 'enableLeakCompensation' is defined, we express the possibility of enabling at any time.
